https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/7c71db049a551da12c977448b66c1037250e6138/R-package/src/Makevars.win.in#L18-L22

Looking at the [test log](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/pull/3692/checks?check_run_id=2383920103) for Django 3.2, it looks like there are a number of deprecation warnings which we need to address (if we don't projects are going to get a lot of warnings that they can't do anything about):

```
RemovedInDjango40Warning: The DEFAULT_HASHING_ALGORITHM transitional setting is deprecated. Support for it and tokens, cookies, sessions, and signatures that use SHA-1 hashing algorithm will be removed in Django 4.0.
```
This is just a test setting which can be safely removed.

```
DeprecationWarning: the imp module is deprecated in favour of importlib; see the module's documentation for alternative uses
```
Looks like we need to switch to importlib.

```
RemovedInDjango40Warning: request.is_ajax() is deprecated. See Django 3.1 release notes for more details about this deprecation.
    if self.request.is_ajax():
```
We're going to have to provide our own implementation of this, as suggested in the Django [release notes](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/3.2/releases/3.1/#id2). 

```
RemovedInDjango40Warning: Passing None for the middleware get_response argument is deprecated.
    super().__init__(get_response)
```
Should be a simple change.

```
RemovedInDjango41Warning: 'oscar.apps.customer' defines default_app_config = 'oscar.apps.customer.apps.CustomerConfig'. However, Django's automatic detection did not find this configuration. You should move the default config class to the apps submodule of your application and, if this module defines several config classes, mark the default one with default = True.
```
Should also be a simple change.

I don't know if you have the time/capacity to address these - if you do then it would be greatly appreciated.

This seems useful, two comments:

- the name sounds to me like the thing being decorated is "quietly experimental" . Recommend changing the name to `@quiet_experimental_warnings`.
- Also, I'm not sure it makes sense to mark a class with this and then only silence warnings in the `__init__`. I realize you are following the pattern of the existing `@experimental` decorator, but in that case we are marking the target itself as experimental, which is not really analogous to what we're doing here. Why not drop the class case and just apply the function version to a class' `__init__`? IMO the effect would be more self explanatory this way.

Hello @hemanth-silabs 

Thank you for this contribution, it's an important step towards more secure products! Good work!

I have an overall question about architecture. Right now the code is sliced with ifdefs in many places to choose between PSA API and mbedTLS API.

Haven't you considered using only the PSA API (whenever possible)? As far as I understand, we can update the mbedTLS version to one that supports PSA API (e.g. 2.25 from TF-M 1.3) - I have it done on a branch and can contribute. Since for non-TF-M type of devices, PSA API implementation translates code to mbedTLS API under the hood anyway. For example the `psa_hash_update` will call `mbedtls_sha256_update_ret` - [code](https://github.com/ARMmbed/mbedtls/blob/v2.25.0/library/psa_crypto.c#L2655). The only caveat is that for such non-TF-M devices, we would need to implement ITS API for the key-by-reference usage.

As an additional argument, I believe mbedTLS will not have "old" mbedTLS API in due time anyway.

Thanks for opening this RFC!

There are a few high-level things that I'm concerned about:
1. The `with_column()` column adding/mutating API isn't vectorized and is a pretty thin layer over `map_batches()`, so it's sure to be much slower than the `map_batches()` equivalent without a large improvement in UX.
2. The fact that each of these add/mutate/select/drop operations results in a separate `map_batches()` execution stage will make it much less efficient than the single `map_batches()` alternative.

For a concrete before/after comparison, here's the preprocessing stages from the big data training CUJ, along with an added bit to drop a column.

## Before RFC

```python
        def batch_transformer(df: pd.DataFrame):
            # Drop columns.
            df = df.drop(columns["bad_col_1", "bad_col_2"])

            # Drop nulls.
            df = df.dropna(subset=["nullable_feature"])

            # Add new column.
            df["new_col"] = (
                df["feature_1"] - 2 * df["feature_2"] + df["feature_3"]
            ) / 3.0

            # Transform column.
            df["feature_1"] = 2.0 * df["feature_1"] + 0.1

            return df

        ds = ds.map_batches(batch_transformer, batch_format="pandas")
```

## After RFC

```python
        ds = ds.drop_columns("bad_col_1", "bad_col_2") \
            .with_column(
                "new_col", lambda r: (r["feature_1"] - 2 * r["feature_2"] + r["feature_3"]) / 3.0) \
            .with_column("feature_1", lambda r: 2.0 * r["feature_1"] + 1) \
            .map_batches(lambda df: df.dropna(subset=["nullable_feature"]), batch_format="pandas")
```

I don't think that the latter is a big improvement in UX, and it will probably end up being a good bit slower. 🤔 

What do you think?

I like it!

I'm happy you have another PR open for the client implementations, so that indeed we can state that the new method is supported everywhere. We need to make sure we land this and the other PR(s) in the same release, so that docs are correct.

We discussed a little over matrix some other ideas, which we decided against. Making a note of our discussion here for our future selves:

1) Possibly stating which release the new method became available in, both for clients and Object Service. Decided against since we don't have a guaranteed way of knowing which release this will make it into, depending on whether another release is made before / after all the PRs are approved and merged.

2) We've decided not to reserve any properties for future expansion (such as having the ability to specify location, so that a region-specific url could be served, in the case of content that is mirrored between various regions/clouds). We decided not to do this, since we were not sure what that might look like, and therefore if additional properties come along, those would be done in a new method (not `getUrl`). We decided setting `additionalProperties: true` would be too loose and could lead to problems with misspellings going unnoticed).

3) We talked about the name `getUrl` and that the `simple` method also returns a url, and that the difference with this method is that it allows you to provide expected hashes and have a content encoding. However, we couldn't agree on a good name that represented these differences with the `simple` method, and since you are getting rid of `simple` we thought `getUrl` was ok and simple.

4) We noted that if you do get rid of `simple` we will need to make sure that where Object Service is already being used (e.g. in external deployments) that the services should be updated before clients, in order that negotiation doesn't fail if client removes `simple` support, but Object Service doesn't yet support `getUrl` method.

I think we can add a more detailed "Troubleshooting out-of-memory errors" guide to the "Performance tips and tuning" page, so this one is just about internal execution model and the guide is more user-facing. We should be pretty prescriptive in the guide, i.e. not just "call map_batches with batch size small enough" but actually walk through an example and show what you need to do.

The guide should cover:
1. Diagnosing out-of-memory error (worker crashes, if there's relevant info in DatasetStats, etc)
2. How to fix it:
- When to use a smaller batch size
- When to decrease target block size (usually unnecessary but it may be required if the machine's RAM:CPU ratio is low)

It may also be good to cover iter_batches here, since the difference between that batch_size vs map_batches batch size seems to be a common source of confusion.

First of all, so exciting to see you back around here @dmontagu! 🤩🎉

## PR Review

Now to the PR review:

I've been reviewing this for the last couple of days. Very clever all around. Amazing job, as always! 🚀

I added a couple of comments here and there. A couple of opinions in the places where I suspect my opinion could be relevant.

## Type Checks from VS Code - PyRight

I noticed a couple of errors with VS Code/PyRight in some places, I think mainly about getting a dict key form a dict where it is not required, or things like that.

Would it be valuable to point them out and suggest the fixes or is it just too much noise on top of the actual PR review?

I'm just adding a couple of those comments for now.

## Questions

About some of the questions in the description, my opinions inline.

Pretty much in favor with everything, for the rest I don't have a strong opinion or enough knowledge.

**Things left to resolve:**

* [ ] Field kwargs
	* [x] Should we remove or keep the 'examples' kwarg on `Field`?
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Re-add the examples keyword argument
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. All things equal, +1 to re-adding it. Unless there's something against it.
	* [ ] Should we remove or keep the 'const' kwarg on `Field`? (This was used to indicate a field was required and the only allowed value was its default.)
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop this argument in favor of `Literal` and/or validators as appropriate (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. If it's the same, I would think it's better to keep one single way to do it, and if there's now a "standard" way to do it with type annotations, I would use that. One single way reduces combinatorial logic, maintenance, testing. And it's easier for users to learn one single thing than two, even more if it's two competing ones.
	* [ ] Should we change the `regex` kwarg to `pattern` in the `Field` signature?
		* **Tentative plan:** Change `regex` to `pattern` (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. As JSON Schema uses the same `pattern` keyword, I would vote for that. `regex` might be more intuitive from Python-land, but for me, `regex` is more ambiguous as to if it takes a regular expression object or a pattern string. Even though the Python docs also refer to a Pattern object, but anyway, I personally associate the word "pattern" more easily with the string.
	* [ ] Do we want to do a backwards-compatibility handler thing with `**kwargs` — warning about now-invalid kwargs (whether or not we keep them working) on `Field`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Do not attempt to maintain backwards compatibility, but document in the migration guide
			* [@tiangolo] **Strongly** in favor with plan. More details below, and also inline on the PR.


* [ ] ConfigDict fields
	* [ ] Do we want to re-add 'schema_extra' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* Either way, do we retain/drop support on a config _class_ in the model definition?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.
	* [ ] Do we want to re-add 'fields' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of annotations and/or `= Field(...)`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.

* [ ] Default values
	* If a default value of a field is not JSON encodable, do we include anything in the schema indicating the default value? Do we error?
	* **Tentative plan:** Just don't include the value in the schema if json encoding fails, but keep the `required` field set appropriately. I believe this is perfectly compatible with JSON schema.
		* [@tiangolo] Soft in favor with plan. I'm not sure about the implications, but I think it should be fine.

* [ ] `__modify_schema__` (-> `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`)
	* Do we retain backwards compatibility with the name `__modify_schema__`, or force migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Force the migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. This is the moment to break changes.
	* Do we support any args/kwargs for `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__` besides the schema itself?
		* Do we pass any info to it from FieldInfo? The CoreSchema? A "source" object? (Could be useful when working with custom generics)
		* **Tentative plan:** Not sure. Would potentially be useful to have access to the core schema and to the type "source" (e.g., to check generic parameter values, etc.)

* [ ] FastAPI
	* Do we need to retain functions analogous to any of the following? (Note we have retained `model_schema`, and `schema`.)
		* get_flat_models_from_field/get_flat_models_from_fields
		* get_flat_models_from_model/get_flat_models_from_models
		* get_model_name_map
		* model_process_schema
	* If not, can we drop their tests?
	* **Tentative plan:** Could we instead build a CoreSchema in FastAPI that produces the desired JSON schema?
		* I'm inclined to put significant effort into helping with the migration of FastAPI to pydantic v2, and to implement any necessary analogous functions as a part of that effort. But if it's already clear that we will need them, happy to add them now.
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. And thanks, as always! 🎉 Better not to add premature optimizations/abstractions. And more details in line in the PR.

* [ ] Callable type
	* How should we handle JSON schema generation for fields with type callable? (See, e.g., `tests.test_schema.test_callable_type`)
	* **Tentative plan:** Not sure.

* [ ] Decimal
	* What should the Decimal schema be?
	* **Tentative plan:** Need to come to a consensus; https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/1511 may be relevant
		* Note: although serialization and parsing are different, I think for FastAPI/OpenAPI purposes it is important that the JSON schema be compatible with the serialization type. (Or else generated clients will produce type errors if an API returns a Decimal.) Happy to discuss this more.
			* [@tiangolo] agreed that incompatible clients would be a non-obvious but important problem. Although I'm not sure I understand the problem well and what's the "serialization type" in this context.

## kwargs in `Field()`

Details about asking for no more kwargs in `Field()`:

I would request to **not** accept any extra kwargs. I asked for that here in this hidden comment: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/4516#discussion_r1010870226

Copying here for context/completeness:

> This `**extra` has been used to add stuff to JSON Schema.

> But in some cases, people pass other stuff here expecting it to be used later or for other purposes, but then it also affects JSON Schema.

> I would like to ask to make it a single keyword argument `extra` instead of `**extra`, or alternatively, to have another single argument `json_schema_extra` so that extensions for JSON Schema can be apart from extra values passed here for other purposes.

> I haven't seen a use for this apart from JSON Schema, and the fact that it makes `Field()` accept any keyword arguments makes editors not complain about arguments that actually do nothing. I've seen this sometimes, e.g. recently with a `Field(required=True)`, but `required` is not a valid argument, if it's required or not is defined by the absence of a `default`. But the code seemed fine and linters passed, because it was taken by the catch all `**extra`.

> It's a breaking change, but I think it's totally worth it and this is the right time for those changes.

## Semi-off topic, highlights

**Note**: no action item here, just praise and highlights of non-obvious extra benefits. 🎉

Very clever implementation! 🚀

I like how the core schema refs, def refs, and json refs are stored and correlated, and then updated when necessary, but still stored. And how everything is stored in the object.

I think this will allow me to do something I've been wanting to do in FastAPI, to pre-generate the JSON Schema stuff for each endpoint, and when there's an error, to show the specific endpoint with an error instead of just saying "something is wrong".

## Wishlist - collect errors

I was checking the wishlist of things I wanted for Pydantic and almost everything is already done. 🎉

From the things not there yet, there's one single thing I would like to ask that is related to JSON Schema, although not sure if it could be a subsequent PR. It's also related to the point above.

### The problem

First the problem.

Currently, an example like this, rightfully breaks:

```Pyhton
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict


class Car:
    def __init__(self, make: str, model: str, year: int):
        self.make = make
        self.model = model
        self.year = year

class Model(BaseModel):
    f1: int = 1
    f2: Car

    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)


Model.model_json_schema()
```

But if `Model` is in a FastAPI path operation, and maybe `Car` is in a sub-model in a sub-field, in a large app, when calling the OpenAPI endpoint the whole thing will break, saying there's a problem with `Car`, but without saying where exactly... and more importantly, none of the OpenAPI will be generated. So, a single broken field breaks the whole OpenAPI and Swagger UI.

### Feature Request

Currently it immediately raises an error like:

```
Cannot generate a JsonSchema for X
```

...that stops the rest of the JSON Schema generation.

I would like instead to accumulate those errors and get them all together, but to be able to retrieve the rest of the JSON Schema.

### Possible Implementation

I think it's better to state the problem in case you see a better way to solve it, but I can also imagine possible implementations. For example:

```Python
schema = Model.model_json_schema()
```

...raises as normally, but:

```Python
schema, errors = Model.model_json_schema(keep_errors=True)
```

Doesn't raise, and returns a JSON Schema where anything with an error is replaced with `{}` and `errors` is a list of stored exceptions. Of course, `keep_errors` could use some bikeshedding on the parameter name, the same for the return interface, but you get the idea.

It could also be a context manager, maybe too complex, but that would keep the API interface (params, return) of `model_json_schema()` unmodified for both cases, don't know. And it would probably only be used by FastAPI and libraries like that, so not too bad on that side. But it would require contextvars and all that dance. E.g.

```Python
with ErrorCollector as c:
	schema = Model.model_json_schema()
print(e.errors)
```

Again, the specific implementation is up for discussion, but I would like to have a way to do this.

### Plan in FastAPI

My plan is also later to put this in FastAPI in a way that (if I manage to) when there's an error like this, the traceback would use Rich and point to the right file and line with the specific FastAPI *path operation* that uses that model. But I think that to be able to do that I first need to pre-compute and collect the JSON Schema errors per path-operation, and not only at the global level.

## Breaking Changes

About the breaking changes, mostly renaming things, in the small few places this happens, I think this is the right moment to do it and discard the cruft of backwards compatibility for those things.

An improvement in that migration, alternative to keep backwards compatibility, would be a tool to automatically update code to use the new things, for the simple ones (I can imagine, e.g. moving kwargs in `Field()` to `json_schema_extra`, or changing `regex` to `pattern`). Maybe that could also work for the `model_config`.

I haven't done it, but I know it's possible and feasible with LibCST. @Kludex has built a couple of CLI tools that do that type of thing, e.g. https://github.com/Kludex/bump-testclient

With raft port being deduced from the REST API port, the change of API port will implicitly change the pysyncobj identifier (host:port), what could create bring the cluster to an unrecoverable state, because on start Patroni checks whether this node is already known as a cluster member and if it is not - adds itself.

Example. We have a cluster from `10.0.0.1:18080,10.0.0.2:18080,10.0.0.3:18080`.
After the change of the REST API port on the first node to 8081, the raft address will become `10.0.0.1:18081`, and it will add itself to the cluster members. The list of members will become `10.0.0.1:18080,10.0.0.2:18080,10.0.0.3:18080,10.0.0.1:18081`. This configuration will stop working as soon as we stop the first node.

For me it looks like a footgun.

I came across another general issue. We currently filter out all unplayable tracks and this means we'll be removing them whenever we make any playlist change, which is wrong. Tracks might be currently unplayable if unavailable in the geo region where the user is logged in (but may be playable from elsewhere and/or in the future). I think local files might also be treated as unplayable but I'm not sure if Spotify still supports this feature I never used it. I think to workaround this we'll need to change the current playlist behaviour (which has been the same since our first implementation) to now include unplayable tracks in playlists. libspotify won't be able to load these tracks when it comes to try and play them and they will be skipped with an `info` log message (which we will want to adjust). These unplayable tracks will clutter the queue/playlist listing and might be confusing to users when they try to play them. But I don't see another way to avoid losing them when we use `save()`.

I've not looked closely at the myers implementation here, I assume it's the normal one that's available everywhere. It seems odd that in 2020 there is no standard, fast (C), robust implementation available for us to just pull in and use. It's not ideal we have to reproduce (and test) our own version of this common algorithm. But we do have Python's standard `difflib.SequenceMatcher`, so what does using myers provide over just using that? I did find multiple (why???) packages for Google's [diff-match-patch](https://github.com/google/diff-match-patch) library. That seems to use many of the optimisations we originally talked about in #236 but I've no great interest in using that, seems overly complicated. 

At a higher level, some of what has been added to playlist.py belongs in web.py. You'll notice there isn't currently any Spotify Web API specific stuff in playlist.py. I think we want to keep that separation.

The `mc_server.py` file should be renamed to something a bit more indicative that it;s going to be holding response classes, for example `status_response.py`

> One option we have to fix those that would not require an API-breaking change would be to make a deep copy of the in-progress Package at each call to Snapshot. This would break pointer equality all over the place, but would at least ensure that we're not tip-toeing around data races.

Even with this option, we still leak the in-progress `*Package` via the iterators, etc. With that in mind, I think that we should merge this as-is, tolerate the data race for the moment (which only arises if a consumer is reading one of the racy fields at the same time it is being published or if the language importer is running while a consumer is reading from language-specific data), and plan on making a breaking change to the APIs that expose the raw `*Package`. It is probably enough to replace the raw `*Package`s with `PackageReference`s, and we should be able to phase the change in s.t. things aren't ever totally broken.

Thanks, have a few comments:

The main place where paths are stored now is `StorageContext`. I've actually had a TODO about how this handles windows and relative paths, so would be a good time to fix it up.

StorageContext takes in the exact user `storage_path` that's passed, as well as an optional `storage_filesystem`. Here's what I was imagining the path handling could look like:

```python
class StorageContext:
    def __init__(self, storage_path: Tuple[str, os.PathLike], storage_filesystem: Optional[pyarrow.fs.FileSystem]):
        if storage_filesystem provided:
            # just convert storage_path to posix path (don't convert to an absolute path)
            # this is because the path should stay relative to the custom_fs
        elif local path (scheme == file or no scheme):
            # expand and convert to *absolute* posix path
        else: # do nothing for URIs, which will get handled by pyarrow.
            assert is_uri(storage_path)
```

I wanted to avoid checking `is_local_path` in this class, but it seems like it's necessary to support relative / windows paths, since pyarrow will raise an error from the input to `pyarrow.fs.from_uri`.

Some nitty suggestions, nothing blocking.

IMO I'd prefer `image_mask` over `pixel_mask` in all cases. If the `image_mask` is numerically sparse, gzip should be able to take care of that. `image_mask` + gzip will probably take up even less space than unzipped `pixel_mask`, especially if the `image_mask` data in binary. If you want to go next level, you could also set the `fill_value` to 0 and only fill in the chunks that contain some of the ROI. I can't think of a good compelling case for using `pixel_mask` over `image_mask`

The tests made me realize that for input data with row names, the value of `reshape` isn't sufficient to determine whether or not the output should have row names.

`reshape`'s value only matters for multi-class classification...for regression and binary classification tasks, the number of rows in the output from `predict()` should be the same as the number of rows in `newdata`, so for those objectives row names should be preserved _regardless of the value of `reshape`_.

* move the code you've added into `predict.lgb.Booster()` into `Predictor$predict()`
* ensure that for binary classification and regression, row names are always preserved

There is no need to reject the request. Converters will take care of everything and they will throw the error if the conversion fails for any reason.
Nopes, we should reject the call at api 36.0. Since we are not required to support forward compatibility in payload. 36.0 specifies payload as RDE 2.0.0, if client provides anything below 2.0.0 or above 3.0.0, the request should be rejected. For 35.0, RDE 1.0.0 is the specification, but the clusters are represented as 2.0.0 in vCD hence the convertor is being used. General rule of thumb, server should try to accept the payload only at the version the api specifies, but is free to do conversion for backend communication with vCD.

I'm a strong :-1: on this for the QtPy 2.0.0 release and believe it should be deferred to QtPy 2.1.0 or later, after the EOL, because:

* We've tested and maintained compatibility all the way up to the release, so it doesn't really make sense to suddenly drop it now and make it impossible for Python 3.6 users to install, despite it being fully functional
* There isn't a compelling benefit to do so immediately; no compat code was simplified/removed and it didn't simplify the CI setup at all (at least as of the current state of the PR); running `pyupgrade --py37-plus` over the full codebase, only one line was changed, and that one only trivially.
* It means all of our tens of thousands of downstream dependencies will either have to drop Python 3.6 support or not upgrade to QtPy 2.0 until they do, and there will be never be a supported combination of Qt6 and Python 3.6
* All of our supported bindings (Pyside2, Pyside6, PyQt5, PyQt6) all still support Python 3.6, so it would be premature to drop it before any of them do
* Checking the [PyPI download stats](https://pypistats.org/packages/qtpy), besides the large spike for 3.7 on Linux (likely driven by CI/automation systems), over the past six months Python 3.6 was a strong number 3 behind Python 3.8 in popularity for QtPy downloads, occasionally even exceeding it.

Furthermore, if we do decide to merge this, there are several specific additional items that need to be investigated/confirmed, and this blocks work on #269 until it is closed or resolved since it touches the same lines.

I strongly recommend that we close this PR for now, retain the existing Python 3.6 support in QtPy 2.0.0, and then drop it in a future 2.1.0 or a later minor version after the EoL, subject to revision if PyQt/PySide dropping support or a non-trivial Py36-only bug crops up that I'm not able to easily solve. I'm happy to commit to doing the work myself when the time comes, including verifying all the skips, at any point in QtPy 2.1.0 or later and after the EoL next month.

Thought about this some more. Posting some comments I made internally here for the record. 

Making manifest.file a list is probably a nonstarter in my opinion:

- Potential impact on `Manifest.from_file()`, `Manifest.from_topdir()`, `Manifest.from_data()`, `Manifest.__init__()` needs to be investigated
- Impact on west manifest subcommands needs to be investigated (this breaks `west manifest --path` for sure -- others?)
- Getting the syntax right needs to be fixed (comma as separator does not work without other changes as I've commented above)
- Precisely specifying the semantics needs to be done still (what if I "git pull" and one or more of the files goes away? any other edge cases?)
- Extending `west init` to support this needs to be investigated (do we just allow `--mf` to be given multiple times?)


The impact on the API is also pretty unclear to me and it looks like there are dealbreakers:

- For example, `west.manifest.Manifest.abspath` is an attribute that contains the absolute path to the manifest file. With multiple, files, this seems broken. Why does this matter? Because you can ask for `west.manifest.Manifest.from_topdir()` to get "the" Manifest object from a workspace topdir, for example.
- Would west need to have to have multiple Manifest objects in flight at any given time in this idea? That would break the `west.commands.WestCommand.manifest` attribute, which both built-in and extension commands rely on to investigate the workspace.

TL;DR west has the assumption that there is one and only one manifest file pretty deeply baked into some core abstractions and I think it is likely that it is too late to change this.

Welcome to LocalStack! Thanks for raising your first Pull Request and landing in your contributions. Our team will reach out with any reviews or feedbacks that we have shortly. We recommend joining our [Slack Community](https://localstack.cloud/contact/) and share your PR on the **#community** channel to share your contributions with us. Please make sure you are following our [contributing guidelines](https://github.com/localstack/.github/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) and our [Code of Conduct](https://github.com/localstack/.github/blob/main/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md).

There's already a linting config for flake8+plugins here which uses the configs defined in setup.cfg:
- https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/05bdf991b367ce8a5f150ec1fd7239c4a7aaff89/.github/workflows/main.yml#L44-L52
- https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/05bdf991b367ce8a5f150ec1fd7239c4a7aaff89/setup.cfg#L12-L53

This linting config is run in the test job, which is a requirement for some of the other jobs to start. Adding another workflow makes it run in parallel without any effects on a failure, especially if you add `|| true` to each shell command.

- Bandit and Safety are an alternative to GH's native CodeQL, which we haven't merged after an earlier attempt that was dismissed because of packaged legacy code that's still included and won't get touched anymore
- Black is very much opinionated and considered "beta". While I don't see much wrong with its style and appreciate a modernization of the code style in general, Streamlink still has a lot of old code with weird styles which was added years ago and hasn't been touched yet.
  Adding "aggressive" linting rules is good for the project maintainers, but it ultimately makes it unwelcoming for new contributors, same with pre-commit hooks (which I hate for this very reason).
- The rules of bugbear are rather meh but it could be added to the already existing linting config, as it's a flake8 plugin.
- Codespell seems unnecessary.
- Pyupgrade is unnecessary and doesn't work well with its default config. I already made this observation when I ported Streamlink's code to 3.6 and dropped 2.7 last year.
- isort is unnecessary because we already have an import order linting config for flake8.

Streamlink also doesn't define its requirements via a `requirements.txt` and running tests again in the linting config doesn't make much sense.

With the addition of a scenario in #4047, I was able to get the following results for this PR (commit 26affbeb50c40454e52b4d88465e670a77c24d08). We see a pretty consistent worst case increase of 7% (2-3 us) when one of the user agent variants is present in the request headers. This a best case increase of 2-3% when there is no match is important. I would conclude that this PR does not introduce a performance regression. 

| Benchmark                                 | 1.3.2 | 1.4.0rc2.dev81+g26affbeb |
|-------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|-----------------------|
| sethttpmeta-no-useragentvariant           | 33.7 us                              | 34.6 us: 1.03x slower |
| sethttpmeta-all-disabled                  | 13.6 us                              | 14.2 us: 1.04x slower |
| sethttpmeta-useragentvariant_exists_1     | 34.4 us                              | 36.7 us: 1.07x slower |
| sethttpmeta-useragentvariant_exists_2     | 37.4 us                              | 40.2 us: 1.07x slower |
| sethttpmeta-useragentvariant_exists_3     | 34.3 us                              | 36.8 us: 1.07x slower |
| sethttpmeta-useragentvariant_not_exists_1 | 34.3 us                              | 35.2 us: 1.03x slower |
| sethttpmeta-useragentvariant_not_exists_2 | 34.5 us                              | 35.3 us: 1.02x slower                       |

I have a few changes here and some minor concerns -- mainly performance (though it's minor). I think repeated calls to `get_origin` and `get_args` leads to unexpected performance consequences. The old code that checked the attribute directly was pretty much free -- however the new `typing` methods have a lot of checks going in them which makes them less than free. I think as a result it's better to save calls and cache those calls as much as possible so they're not repeatedly called.

ok.

1) as we are forking the implementation code and copying from the non async io code path, we need to have a plan on maintenance. now if there is any bug let's say in the query stack we need to fix this into different codebase.

multiple options to consider for future:

* make the sync api a shim layer on top of async API, rather than having two separate implementations
* deprecated the sync API

2. API `self.client_connection.last_response_headers` may cause indeterministic behaviour, as for example in the context of cross partition query or when there is any concurrency it is not possible to identify really which header response associates with which set of result

3. I don't see any test for the new async API added. Did we add test?

4. in general we should try to follow python culture but at the same time be close as possible to the existing SDKs, you can do a quick cross check between the new python api and the java sdk.
few deviations:
- in java v4 we don't explicitly need to enable cross partition query, (we shouldn't need explicit `enable_cross_partition_query`)
